comment_text.ud7.7:1.3: “Judgment” (saṅkhā) refers to the way we “appraise” or “assess” ourselves, especially in relation to others (cf. MN 1:3.3, DN 1:1.3.2). | “Proliferation” (papañca) is the compulsion of the mind to spread out in endless inner commentary that hides reality. | “Perceptions” refer to the underlying tendencies that cause us to interpret the present in terms of the past. See MN 18 for a detailed explanation.

root_text.ud7.7:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.ud7.7:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā attano papañcasaññāsaṅkhāpahānaṁ paccavekkhamāno nisinno hoti.
root_text.ud7.7:2.1: Atha kho bhagavā attano papañcasaññāsaṅkhāpahānaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

translation_text.ud7.7:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.ud7.7:1.3: Now at that time the Buddha was sitting reviewing his own giving up of the judgments driven by the proliferation of perceptions.
translation_text.ud7.7:2.1: Then, understanding his own giving up of the judgments driven by the proliferation of perceptions, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.ud7.7:3.1: “There is no proliferation remaining in those
translation_text.ud7.7:3.2: who have passed beyond the halter and cross-bar.
translation_text.ud7.7:3.3: The sage who lives without craving
translation_text.ud7.7:3.4: is never scorned by the world with its gods.”
